posts.cooked:
If you could only drink from 2 houses, what would they be?

Assuming price was no issue, but also doesn’t have to be the driving factor…and why?

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Taittinger and Delamotte. Hard to leave out Ruinart.

Why? Love CDC and it is a great value for the quality.

Delamotte’s 2008 is so good and an excellent value while Salon is just great.

posts.name: Andy Steinman
posts.cooked: Cedric Bouchard and Vilmart with Bereche reluctantly left out

posts.name: Mike Silverman
posts.cooked: Taittinger and Krug

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Krug because no one does the style better. Bouchard would be my initial choice for 2 but maybe Marguet due to the fact that there are so many good choices in the lineup. All the vineyard wines would keep things interesting year to year.

Can I count LVMH as a house?

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
Krug…so much in my wheelhouse! Could you imagine to be able to drink ALL five of those wines daily!

DP…such iconic wine…don’t even need the others produced by Moet.

posts.name: Ed Steinway
posts.cooked: Roederer and Krug. Roederer also for domestic sparkling and for their champagne.

Roederer, because both the domestic and imported line up is solid at all price points, and, Cedric Bouchard, because he made the bubbly I liked the best.
Best, Jim

I agree with this completely. Roederer has a top end that competes with anyone (and is getting stronger by the year), a mid range that is absolutely on fire right now, both white and rose, and a low end (Champagne and CA) that is miles better than most of the competition. No other producer I know of can hit all the price points and all the sub-types I like. Bouchard is as good as any brand to fill gaps (although the prices have gotten borderline too high). Vilmart and Don Ruinart are worthy also-rans

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
If my “only 2” champagne producers made wines that started at $125 and $350 respectively, I supposed I’d have to find something other than champagne to drink.

Nothing in the OP’s post prevents you from drinking Cava, Sext, California sparkling wine, sparkling wines from the Loire or still wines from everywhere in the world.

If my “only 2” champagne producers made wines that started at $125 and $350 respectively, I supposed I’d have to find something other than champagne to drink.

Nothing in the OP’s post prevents you from drinking Cava, Sext, California sparkling wine, sparkling wines from the Loire or still wines from everywhere in the world.

Remember the old R.I.F. acronym?

[/quote

Remember the old R.I.F. acronym?

When and if you learn better reading comprehension skills, you might realize that the point of my post is that giving up less expensive Champagnes is really not a horrible hardship given all the many varied wines, including sparkling wines, in the world and that it is perfectly reasonable for someone to want to retain drinking their two favorites and give up lesser Champagnes when there are so many excellent alternatives out there.
This was fun to read through. Thanks for the discussion.

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked:
Richard, funny reply.

Dominique farms biodynamic from a slope in Polisot, which is in the south nor far from Chablis. The wines are all non-dose, yet what I find with these consistently is a beautiful fruit presence and balance. I continue to load these in my cellar, typically at 3 bottles of each cuvee…they are to me that good. For what it’s worth, Dominique’s husband, Roland Piollot, also has a label, and his wines are from around the village and area, but not from the slope where Dominique farms for her bottlings. Howard, you should look into these wines…they are excellent.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey

I’m surprised that Pol Roger doesn’t show up more. It would come in at #3 behind Krug and Vilmart for me. I find Pol Roger so delicious all the way up and down the line, with its normal and BdB vintage wines being particularly noteworthy for their levels.

Interesting question. Roederer would definitely be one, as the quality is superb up and down the range (and there is quite a range). Maybe Philipponnat?

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked: Steak or heavier beef like prime rib? Not doubting, just curious what one could pair with boeuf.

Pat_Martin:

Steak or heavier beef like prime rib? Not doubting, just curious what one could pair with boeuf.

Of course I have a bunch of reds that I would choose first, but I would be happy with, eg, a Jacquesson Terres Rouge or a Larmandier Bernier Rose de Saignee with beef. I am not a person who obsesses about pairings. If I like the dish and I like the wine, I will usually be able to make do

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked: I find that BdB or a Chard/Pinot blend with a bit of dosage (say 3-7 or 8 g/l so not Brut Nature) masterfully pairs with steak. Think NY Strip or Rib Eye. It’s a killer match. So is Demisec Chenin. Honestly I’ve had better symbiosis with white pairings like this than red wines. The rapier acidity cuts through the richness and fat and that acidity and the bit of sugar create a flavor bomb in the mouth.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
Cool. I need to try this.

I still recall the amazement in the room with my local group when once upon a time we had a Riesling Spatlese pair perfectly with carne cruda.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence

Yep!

Just to clarify that Taittinger is really just Comtes for me, but Roederer includes Cristal, Roederer vintage and Collection. I have not had Starck so can’t opine on that. Even Roederer Estate is very good for a US sparkler. Has any other House consistently been making better Champagne than Roederer this decade?
